concerns.text: The plan validates the Domino framework's speedup claim (5.49x) by comparing it against a result obtained on a 0.5B parameter model (Qwen2-0.5B) on CPU. The paper's claim is specific to Qwen3 (likely >7B) on GPU. Speedup in speculative decoding is highly non-linear with respect to model size and hardware architecture (memory bandwidth vs. compute). Validating a GPU/large-model claim using a CPU/tiny-model proxy is a category error; the result is not an empirical test of the paper's claim but a test of a different system entirely. The 'validation' cannot confirm or refute the paper's specific claim.

The plan defines the validation target as 'speedup against a baseline' on the *same* hardware (CPU). While this measures relative algorithmic efficiency, the plan explicitly attempts to compare this against the paper's absolute speedup claim (5.49x). This comparison is scientifically invalid because the baseline performance (autoregressive decoding) scales differently on CPU vs. GPU than the speculative decoding overhead does. The 'tolerance threshold' (20%) mentioned in FR-007 is meaningless when the underlying physics of the computation (latency bottlenecks) have changed from the source claim.

The schema defines `speedup_ratio` as a single float. For a scientifically sound benchmark, the plan must capture the distribution of speedups across prompts (mean, std dev, min, max) rather than a single aggregate. Speculative decoding performance is highly variance-dependent on the 'acceptance rate' of the draft model, which fluctuates per prompt. A single aggregate ratio hides the statistical validity of the result.
